---
title: "Quarto Fundamentals"
format: html
---

A {{< meta glossary.qmd >}} file must include a YAML header with metadata. This ensures the output is correctly structured and key details about the publication are machine readable. There are tools, such as the Quarto visual editor in RStudio which can help produce your YAML.

The body of the .qmd file typically includes:

-   Narrative text;

-   Code chunks (R, Python, or other languages);

-   Outputs such as figures, tables, or inline calculations.

### Text

Blocks of text are written in plain text which can be done in any text editor - we are using RStudio Source and Visual editors in combination. The visual style is determined by templates and can be edited using a .css stylesheet. To put text into *italics* single asterisks should bracket the character(s) or word(s) like this: `*italics*`. Similarly, **bold** text should be bracketed by double asterisks, like this: `**bold**`. This is not necessary if you use the RStudio Visual editor which does this work for you.

Creators may apply first `#`, second `##`, third `###` order headings (and up to six) by starting a new line with the relevant number of hashes and a space before the text.

### Chunks

Chunks use three backticks ```` ``` ```` bracketing them. Parentheses determine the coding language and controls in place e.g. `{r, eval=FALSE}`.

Chunk options control what learners can see:

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## echo

Controls whether the **code itself is shown** in the output.

-   `echo= TRUE` → shows the code and its output\
-   `echo= FALSE` → hides the code, shows only the results

**Why this matters:**\
Use `echo= FALSE` when you want learners to focus on interpretation of results rather than the underlying code, or when the code is repetitive or advanced.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## eval

Controls whether the code chunk is **executed when the document renders**.

-   `eval= TRUE` → code runs and produces output\
-   `eval= FALSE` → code is shown (if `echo= TRUE`) but not run

**Why this matters:**\
Useful when demonstrating code without running it, for example when the output depends on external data, long computations, or when you want learners to run it themselves.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## warning

Controls whether **warnings generated by the code** are displayed.

-   `warning= TRUE` → warnings are shown\
-   `warning= FALSE` → warnings are suppressed

**Why this matters:**\
Warnings can be helpful for debugging, but hiding them can make outputs cleaner in teaching materials where the warning is not relevant to the learning goal.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## message

Controls whether **informational messages** (e.g. package loading messages) are displayed.

-   `message= TRUE` → messages are shown\
-   `message= FALSE` → messages are hidden

**Why this matters:**\
Messages can clutter output (e.g. `library()` calls). Suppressing them helps keep the focus on key results while maintaining reproducibility behind the scenes.
:::

### Figures, tables, and inline calculations

We strongly suggest using the RStudio Visual editor for figures and tables. This editor will organise your directory, as well as give you controls to add elements such as alternative text. Formatting and organising Figures and tables from scratch can lead to wasted time, even if you have to edit the code after using the Visual editor, you should use this feature unless you are confident with Quarto.

For inline calculations or the production of figures and graphs, simply load the relevant packages and run the code as usual in text. You can use the backticks \` and controls used above to determine if you show the learner the underpinning code, or the output.

[![](images/clipboard-2765196364.png){fig-align="center" width="50"}](/Modular%20OERs/Modular-Useful features.html)
